2.16: A revelation of God: 'My beloved is mine, and I am his.' The Song's vision of mutual possession between lover and beloved is the OT's most intimate description of the covenant relationship. In the NT, this mutual possession is fulfilled in the Christ-church relationship: 'You are not your own, for you were bought with a price' (1 Cor 6:19-20); 'I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine' becomes 'I live, and yet not I, but Christ lives in me' (Gal 2:20). The mutual knowing of bride and groom (I know my sheep and my sheep know me, John 10:14) is the new covenant's personal form of the Song's mutual possession. The eschatological fulfillment is the marriage of the Lamb (Rev 19:7-9; 21:2): the Song's vision of complete love is consummated in the new creation when the Bride has made herself ready.
2.1: A shadow: 'I am a wildflower of Sharon, a lily of the valleys.' The bride's self-description is modest — a common wildflower, not a cultivated ornament. The NT's Christological reading sees here the humility of Christ who 'made himself nothing, taking the form of a servant' (Phil 2:7). He is not the rose in the formal garden but the flower of the field — accessible to anyone, everywhere. The church that follows him shares this same lowly beauty in the world's eyes.
2.2: A revelation of God: 'Like a lily among thorns, so is my beloved among the young women.' The beloved is beautiful precisely in contrast to what surrounds her — thorns cannot diminish the lily's beauty but only throw it into relief. The church's beauty in the world follows the same logic: surrounded by the darkness of sin, the church's distinctiveness is her very loveliness. Christ sees his people as beautiful even in a world that cannot see them that way.
2.3: A shadow: The beloved is like an apple tree among the trees of the forest — providing shade and fruit, not merely beauty. 'With great delight I sat in his shade, and his fruit was sweet to my taste.' John 6:35 (I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger) and John 15:5 (I am the vine; whoever abides in me bears much fruit) develop the same nourishment-and-shade imagery. Christ is shade from the heat of judgment (Isa 25:4) and fruit that satisfies the deepest hunger.
2.4: A shadow: 'He brought me to the banquet hall, and his banner over me was love.' The banquet hall (beit hayayin, house of wine) is the place of celebration and feasting — the OT image of eschatological blessing (Isa 25:6; 55:1-2). The banner of love — a military standard planted over conquered territory — declares the territory of the beloved as belonging to the lover. Paul's exposition of Christ's love (Eph 3:17-19: the love that surpasses knowledge) is the banner that covers the church. The eschatological fulfillment is the wedding feast of the Lamb (Rev 19:9).
2.5: A revelation of God: 'Strengthen me with raisins; revive me with apples — for I am faint with love.' The faintness is not distress but the overwhelming excess of love — the soul undone by the intensity of the Beloved's love. Paul's prayer in Ephesians 3:14-19 uses the same structure: he prays that believers 'may have strength to comprehend ... the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge' — knowing that its full comprehension is beyond human capacity. The soul strengthened by the fruits of the Spirit (Gal 5:22) is the NT's form of 'strengthen me with raisins.'
2.6: A revelation of God: 'His left arm is under my head, and his right arm holds me close.' The complete embrace: the lover supports and holds simultaneously. The image of divine arms beneath and around is the OT's expression of omnipresent care (Deut 33:27: 'the eternal God is your dwelling place, and underneath are the everlasting arms'). Christ's care for the church is not from a distance but an embrace — the Good Shepherd lays down his life for the sheep (John 10:11) and carries them (Luke 15:5).
2.7: A revelation of God: The adjuration not to awaken love prematurely — 'do not rouse or awaken love until it pleases' — is the divine sovereignty over timing in the covenant relationship. The kairos of grace cannot be forced; intimacy with God comes on his schedule. The NT parallel is 'do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God, by whom you were sealed' (Eph 4:30) — the Spirit of love is not to be coerced or manipulated. The covenant relationship operates on divine initiative and divine timing throughout.
2.8: A type: 'Listen — it is my beloved! Here he comes, leaping across the mountains, bounding over the hills.' The approach of the beloved from a distance, coming quickly and eagerly — this is the structure of the incarnation and the parousia. The Word who was with God 'leapt' into the world at the incarnation (Wis 18:15 uses this language for the divine Word entering Egypt; the image is ancient). The parousia is described in similar terms: 'the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command' (1 Thess 4:16). Christ comes; he does not merely wait.
2.9: A revelation of God: The beloved stands behind the wall, peering through the windows, gazing through the lattice. The image of the divine observer attentive to the beloved from just beyond sight is the OT's picture of the omniscient God who sees without being fully seen (Ps 139:1-4; Heb 4:13: no creature is hidden from his sight). Christ's attentiveness to the church is complete even when he seems absent (Matt 28:20: I am with you always). He sees before he speaks.
2.10: A shadow: 'Get up, my love, my beautiful one, and come with me.' The lover's call to arise and come is the most urgent and tender of invitations. In the NT, 'come' is the repeated word of the gospel: Jesus's first words to the disciples are 'come and you will see' (John 1:39); 'come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden' (Matt 11:28); the Spirit and the Bride say 'Come' (Rev 22:17). The risen Christ calls the church to arise from death and come — the resurrection as the ultimate 'get up, come with me.'
2.11: A shadow: 'The winter is past; the rains are over and gone.' The seasonal announcement — winter ended, the harsh season past — is a picture of the transition from the old age to the new. The NT uses the same seasonal imagery: 'the night is far gone; the day is at hand' (Rom 13:12). In Christ, the long winter of exile and judgment (Lam 1:1-2) has passed; the new creation spring has begun (2 Cor 5:17: if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; the old has passed away; behold, the new has come).
2.12: A shadow: 'Flowers appear all across the land; the season of singing has arrived, and the voice of the turtledove is heard in our land.' The new-creation catalogue: flowers, singing, the turtledove's voice — all signs that the waiting is over and the renewal has come. The NT's language of new creation (2 Cor 5:17; Gal 6:15; Rev 21:5: 'Behold, I am making all things new') draws on this imagery. The age of lament is ended; the age of the Spirit's song has begun. The turtledove is associated with the Spirit's presence (Matt 3:16: the Spirit descended like a dove).
2.13: A shadow: The fig tree ripening, the vines blossoming — the specific fruits of the promised land (Deut 8:8: a land of vines and fig trees and pomegranates). The lover's invitation to 'come away' repeats the call to participate in the new-creation feast. Jesus uses the fig tree as a sign of the kingdom's coming (Matt 24:32: when its branch becomes tender and puts out its leaves, you know that summer is near); the vine is explicitly identified with himself (John 15:1). The Song's seasonal signs are the signs of Christ's kingdom.
2.14: A revelation of God: 'My dove, hidden in the clefts of the rock — let me see your face and hear your voice; for your voice is sweet, and your face is lovely.' The rock-cleft is Moses's hiding place when the divine glory passed (Exod 33:22); it is the place of protection and intimacy. The lover wants to see the face that is hidden in the shelter he provides. Christ desires the full response of his people: 'your voice is sweet' — the prayers of the saints are sweet incense before God (Rev 5:8; 8:3-4).
2.15: A revelation of God: 'Catch the little foxes that ruin the vineyards — our vineyards are in bloom.' The vines are in bloom — the most vulnerable moment, when the fruit is just forming. The little foxes that destroy are the small sins, the subtle compromises, the 'little things' that damage the believer's relationship with Christ when it is most vulnerable. Paul's warning against allowing 'a little leaven' to leaven the whole lump (1 Cor 5:6; Gal 5:9) is the NT's version of this counsel: vigilance in the season of growth.
2.17: A shadow: 'Until the day breaks and the shadows disappear, turn, my beloved — be like a gazelle or a young stag on the rugged mountains.' The waiting — the night still present, the day not yet arrived — is the church's condition between the resurrection and the parousia. 'The day is at hand' (Rom 13:12) but not yet fully present. The lover is asked to turn and come quickly — the church's prayer 'Come, Lord Jesus' (Rev 22:20; 1 Cor 16:22: Maranatha). The mountains are rugged but the gazelle covers them easily: nothing delays the return of Christ when the time comes.

8.6: A direct revelation: 'Love is strong as death, jealousy is fierce as the grave; its flashes are flashes of fire, the very flame of the LORD.' The Song declares love's unconquerable strength in the face of the two most formidable opponents — death and sheol. The NT's claim is that this declaration is literally, not merely poetically, true in Christ: his love has conquered death (1 Cor 15:54-57: Death is swallowed up in victory; thanks be to God who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ) and the love of God in Christ is literally unconquerable (Rom 8:38-39: neither death nor life ... shall be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord). What the Song celebrates as love's aspiration, the resurrection announces as love's accomplished fact.
8.1: A direct revelation: ‘O that you were like a brother to me.’ The Incarnation is the answer to the exact longing of this verse: Christ became the church’s ‘brother’ in the most complete sense, so that she could embrace him publicly without shame. Heb 2:11-12 is the fulfillment: ‘he is not ashamed to call them brothers, saying, “I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise.”’ The social shame that the beloved feared (being looked down upon for public embrace) is precisely what Christ absorbs in the crucifixion: ‘despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows’ (Isa 53:3). The shame that social convention threatened, Christ took; the brotherhood the beloved longed for, Christ became.
8.2: A type: ‘I would bring you into my mother’s house’ — the deepest hospitality, offering the most intimate domestic space to the beloved. The Lord’s Supper is the church’s bringing of Christ into her house — the table set with the spiced wine and the bread that Christ provided. ‘Behold, I stand at the door and knock. If anyone opens the door, I will come in to him and eat with him, and he with me’ (Rev 3:20). The beloved’s offering of pomegranate nectar and spiced wine to the one she brings into her house is the church’s eucharistic act — she receives the Bridegroom’s gift and offers her own thanksgiving in return.
8.3: A type: the left-hand-under-the-head and right-hand embrace — the total protective posture — is the church’s experience of Christ’s holding of her. Deut 33:27: ‘the eternal God is your dwelling place, and underneath are the everlasting arms.’ John 10:28-29: ‘I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand. My Father, who has given them to me, is greater than all, and no one is able to snatch them out of the Father’s hand.’ The Song’s intimate embrace is the physical image of the theological guarantee of perseverance: the church is held from below (the left hand) and surrounded (the right hand).
8.4: A type: the adjuration not to stir up love until it pleases is the church’s posture of waiting for the parousia. Jas 5:7-8: ‘Be patient, therefore, brothers, until the coming of the Lord. See how the farmer waits for the precious fruit of the earth, being patient about it, until it receives the early and the late rains. You also, be patient. Establish your hearts, for the coming of the Lord is at hand.’ The love that ‘cannot be forced’ is the eschatological love that arrives in its own time — and the church that has experienced its fullness knows not to try to manufacture what only God can give. The patience of the refrain is the patience of the eschatological people.
8.5: A type: ‘Who is this coming up from the wilderness, leaning on her beloved?’ The Incarnation is God coming ‘up from the wilderness’ of human history, and the church who leans on him is already being carried by his strength. Isa 40:31: ‘they who wait for the LORD shall renew their strength; they shall mount up with wings like eagles.’ The apple tree under which the beloved was born — and under which his mother bore him — is the specific place of new life; Christ was born in the lowliest of places (the manger, like a hidden apple tree in the wilderness) so that the church could find her beginning under his shade. The one who comes up from the wilderness is also the one who was tempted in the wilderness (Matt 4:1-11) and came through it to begin his public ministry of love.
8.7: A direct revelation: ‘Many waters cannot quench love, neither can floods drown it. If a man offered all the wealth of his house for love, it would be utterly scorned.’ The flood-chaos imagery (many waters, rivers) represents the full force of opposition — persecution, death, the powers arrayed against the church. Christ’s love for the church has passed through the flood-chaos of the cross and emerged unquenched: ‘neither death nor life, nor angels nor rulers, nor things present nor things to come, nor powers, nor height nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord’ (Rom 8:38-39). The contempt for the man who tries to buy love with wealth is the Song’s rejection of the commercial model of salvation — grace cannot be purchased, only received.
8.8: A type: the brothers’ care for the little sister not yet developed anticipates Christ’s protection of the immature and small in his kingdom. ‘See that you do not despise one of these little ones. For I tell you that in heaven their angels always see the face of my Father who is in heaven’ (Matt 18:10). The brothers who deliberate about what to do for their sister before she is marriageable represent the care of the community for the young and vulnerable — a care that Christ himself models: he rebukes the disciples for turning away children (Matt 19:14) and declares that the kingdom belongs to such. The church’s care for her little ones is the echo of the brothers’ protective love.
8.9: A type: the wall/door discernment — responding to what the beloved is with appropriate care — is the pattern of Christ’s pastoral knowledge of the church. Rev 2-3 (the letters to the seven churches) shows Christ applying exactly this discernment: to Smyrna (strong under persecution) he gives honor; to Laodicea (lukewarm) he offers the correction that secures. Christ’s knowledge of his church is complete and particular: he does not apply the same pastoral response to every situation but responds to what each church actually is. The grace that fortifies the wall and secures the door is the grace of the divine Physician who knows the condition of each patient.
8.10: A direct revelation: ‘I am a wall, and my breasts are like towers; thus I have become in his eyes as one who finds shalom.’ The church that is a wall — firm in faith, not dissolved by pressure — becomes in Christ’s eyes the bearer of his own peace. Christ is the Prince of Peace (sar shalom, Isa 9:6); the church that embodies his character of integrity becomes the instrument of his shalom. Phil 4:7: ‘the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus.’ The guarding-peace of God is the tower that the church becomes in Christ’s eyes when she stands firm. The shalom/shlomoh wordplay is christological: the beloved who finds shalom finds it through her union with the Solomon-Bridegroom who is himself Peace.
8.11: A type: Solomon’s vineyard at Baal-Hamon, leased to keepers for silver, is the image of the world-vineyard managed by imperfect stewards — the parable of the wicked tenants (Matt 21:33-41). Christ is the owner of the vineyard (John 15:1: ‘I am the true vine, and my Father is the vinedresser’) who has entrusted the care of his vineyard to the church. The thousand silver pieces due to Solomon anticipate the accountability that the parable foregrounds: the servants who were given the vineyard must account for what it produced. The church is the faithful tenant who returns not just the rent but the fruit itself.
8.12: A type: ‘My own vineyard is before me; the thousand are for you, O Solomon.’ The church’s free self-gift — ‘my vineyard is mine to give, and I give it to you’ — is the mirror of Christ’s own free self-giving: ‘No one takes [my life] from me, but I lay it down of my own accord’ (John 10:18). The reciprocity is the heart of the covenant: Christ gives himself freely for the church (Eph 5:25), and the church responds by freely giving herself to Christ (2 Cor 8:5: ‘they gave themselves first to the Lord’). The vineyard that is freely given anticipates the New Jerusalem where all things are freely given: ‘to the thirsty I will give from the spring of the water of life without payment’ (Rev 21:6).
8.13: A type: ‘O you who dwell in the gardens, with companions listening for your voice — let me hear it.’ Christ’s desire to hear the church’s voice is the motive for prayer: he invites, even requests, the voice of the one he loves. The companions listening are the cosmic witnesses — the angels who ‘long to look’ into the gospel (1 Pet 1:12) and gather around the worship of the church to hear the voice of the people God has redeemed. The church’s worship is a cosmic event heard by the heavenly assembly: ‘you have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem, and to innumerable angels in festal gathering’ (Heb 12:22). Every time the church gathers to pray, her voice goes out to a gallery that extends beyond what she can see.
8.14: A direct revelation: ‘Flee, my beloved, and be like a gazelle or a young stag on the mountains of spices.’ The Song’s final line is the church’s cry for the parousia. The beloved running swiftly over the fragrant mountains is the return of Christ that the whole NT ends with: ‘Amen. Come, Lord Jesus!’ (Rev 22:20). The invitation to flight/swift return — berach dodi (flee, my beloved) — is not dismissal but the urgency of longing: come quickly. The Song that began with ‘let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth’ (1:2) ends with the beloved in motion, still coming. The incompleteness of the ending is the ‘not yet’ of the eschatological kingdom: the Bridegroom is on his way, and the Bride waits and calls for his coming.

7.1: A type: the Shulammite is called out of her dancing to be seen — the invitation to appear before the bridegroom who celebrates her. Christ calls his church out of hiddenness and shame into the visibility of his own approval: 'You are the light of the world. A city set on a hill cannot be hidden' (Matt 5:14). The machola ha-machanayim (dance of two camps) invokes the Mahanaim of Gen 32 — the site of Jacob’s encounter with the angel-army of God. The beloved who dances between two heavenly camps is the church who belongs to two worlds — she dances at the intersection of earth and heaven, already held by the one who crossed from heaven to earth to find her.
7.2: A type: the bridegroom’s detailed description of the beloved’s body as the site of abundance — the navel like a bowl of wine, the belly like a mound of wheat — speaks to his delight in the whole person God has made. Christ’s love for the church is not a spiritual abstraction that ignores her embodied existence; it is the love of the Creator for what he made and declared good (Gen 1:31). The body is the temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Cor 6:19), and Christ’s declaration of the body’s beauty is the theological ground for bodily resurrection: the same body he declares beautiful, he will raise.
7.3: A type: the repeated twin-fawns image (cf. 4:5) is the Song’s characteristic repetition — the same praise spoken again with fresh delight. Christ’s love for the church does not diminish through familiarity; it speaks the same declarations of beauty from generation to generation. The pastoral imagery (gazelle, twins, fawns) places the beloved in the created order that Christ made and entered: the Good Shepherd who knows his own (John 10:14) delights in them with the same fresh attentiveness as a shepherd delights in young animals in the spring.
7.4: A type: the church’s neck like an ivory tower speaks to the unyielding faithfulness of the church’s witness — the community that does not bend to cultural pressure. The eyes like the pools of Heshbon describe the depth and clarity of faith that sees clearly. Christ commissions this quality in his church: ‘let your eyes be healthy so that your whole body is full of light’ (Matt 6:22). The nose facing Damascus — the city of Paul’s conversion and of the Gentile mission — is the prophetic posture of the church directed outward toward the world she is called to serve.
7.5: A type: the head like Carmel (the majestic promontory) and the flowing locks that capture the king speak to the church’s dignity and the paradoxical power of faith over Christ: he who ‘holds all things together’ (Col 1:17) is ‘captured’ by the church’s beauty — a beauty he himself gave her (Eph 5:26-27). This is the kenosis reversed: the one who emptied himself and humbled himself (Phil 2:7-8) is now, from his position of exaltation, captivated by the church he redeemed. The purple locks (the royal color) placed on the beloved by the bridegroom’s love are the royal priesthood that Christ confers on the church (1 Pet 2:9).
7.6: A type: ‘How fair and pleasant you are, O love, with all your delights!’ is the summary declaration of the bridegroom’s delight in the beloved. Christ’s delight in the church is the love that preceded the cross — he endured the cross ‘for the joy set before him’ (Heb 12:2), and the church is part of that joy. Zeph 3:17 provides the fullest OT statement of this divine delight: ‘The LORD your God is in your midst, a mighty one who will save; he will rejoice over you with gladness; he will quiet you by his love; he will exult over you with loud singing.’ The Song’s bridegroom-delight is the experiential image of the divine rejoicing-over that Zephaniah proclaims.
7.7: A type: the beloved’s stature like a date palm is the image of the church planted, rooted, and bearing fruit. Ps 92:12 declares: ‘the righteous flourish like the palm tree.’ The church is the community of the righteous planted by Christ; her fruit clusters (Eph 5:9, the fruit of light) are what the Bridegroom comes to taste. The palm tree is also the tree of victory — the palm branches of the triumphal entry (John 12:13) and of the great multitude who stand before the throne (Rev 7:9). The beloved-as-palm-tree is the church whose dignity Christ proclaims.
7.8: A type: ‘I say I will climb the palm tree and lay hold of its fruit-clusters.’ The bridegroom’s declaration of intentional pursuit is the Incarnation-movement translated into the language of love: Christ came to ‘lay hold’ of the church (Heb 2:16: ‘he does not lay hold of angels but of the offspring of Abraham’). The same Greek verb epilambanomai (to take hold of) appears in Heb 2:16 and describes precisely the incarnational reaching: God extended his hand into creation and took hold of human flesh in order to take hold of human persons. The climbing and reaching of Song 7:8 is the poetry that Hebrews makes literal.
7.9: A type: the mouth of the beloved like the best wine going down smoothly — the reciprocity of love in which the church’s prayer is heard as the most exquisite speech by Christ, who desires to hear her voice (cf. 2:14, ‘let me hear your voice, for your voice is sweet’). Christ invites the church into the communication of prayer: ‘ask, and it will be given to you’ (Matt 7:7); ‘abide in me, and ask whatever you wish’ (John 15:7). The beloved’s voice that the bridegroom savors as wine is the church’s prayer received by Christ as the most precious thing she offers.
7.10: A direct revelation: ‘I am my beloved’s, and his desire is for me.’ The word teshuqah (desire/longing) here reverses Gen 3:16’s distorted desire: where the fall introduced a desire entangled with domination, the Song restores the desire that is pure self-giving love. Christ’s desire for the church is the redemptive desire that is enacted in the cross: he desired to redeem what was lost, and ‘for the joy set before him he endured the cross’ (Heb 12:2). His desire brought him through death for the church. The beloved’s declaration ‘his desire is for me’ is the church’s assurance that she is the specific object of Christ’s redemptive longing — not an afterthought but the goal of the Incarnation.
7.11: A type: ‘Come, my beloved, let us go out into the fields.’ The beloved’s invitation to Christ to go with her into the world is the church’s missionary movement. The fields (sadeh) in the parable of the sower (Matt 13:38) are the world; the church goes into that world not alone but with the Bridegroom: ‘Go therefore and make disciples of all nations… and behold, I am with you always’ (Matt 28:19-20). The church’s invitation to Christ to come with her into the harvest-field is the prayer that precedes mission. The Lord of the harvest goes with the laborers he sends.
7.12: A type: the vineyard inspection — checking whether the vine has sprouted, the blossoms opened, the pomegranates flowered — is the church’s examination of her own fruitfulness before the return of the Bridegroom. Christ inspects the churches (Rev 2-3, the letters to the seven churches) with the same care the bridegroom brings to the vineyard: what is bearing fruit, what needs pruning, what has not yet bloomed. The beloved’s declaration ‘there I will give you my love’ is the church’s self-offering in the place of productive service — she gives herself most fully in the context of fruitfulness.
7.13: A type: the mandrakes giving fragrance and the precious fruits stored up — new and old — for the beloved represent the church’s accumulated offering to Christ. The early church leader who brings ‘out of his treasure what is new and what is old’ (Matt 13:52) is the steward of the household of God who has stored both the ancient Scriptures and the new revelation of Christ. The church’s all-things-for-you is the participation in the Spirit’s work of preparing the Bride: ‘the Spirit and the Bride say, “Come”’ (Rev 22:17) — and the Bride comes with her hands full of gifts for the Bridegroom.

1.1: A shadow: The superlative title shir hashirim — the Song of Songs — marks this as the peak of the wisdom tradition's meditation on love. Its canonical placement among the wisdom books signals that the love it describes is not peripheral to Israel's theology but central to it. The NT's bridegroom theology (John 3:29; Matt 9:15; Eph 5:25-32; Rev 19:7-9) draws on this tradition: Christ is the bridegroom whose love for the church the Song shadows. The Song anticipates not an abstraction but a person.
1.2: A revelation of God: The Bride's longing — 'Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth' — is the soul's cry for direct intimacy with God, not mediated through law or intermediary but immediate and personal. Paul's prayer in Ephesians 3:17-19 is the NT's answer: that Christ may dwell in your hearts through faith, that you may know the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge. The longing the Song voices is the longing the Spirit creates in every believer (Rom 8:26-27).
1.3: A shadow: 'Your name is like perfume poured out' — the name of the beloved is not merely a title but an essence, a fragrance that draws. The NT's fulfillment is the name of Jesus: 'God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name' (Phil 2:9). The fragrance metaphor returns in 2 Corinthians 2:15: 'we are the aroma of Christ to God.' The young women's love drawn by the name anticipates the elect drawn to Christ (John 6:44: no one can come to me unless the Father draws him).
1.4: A shadow: 'Draw me, let us run' — the initiative belongs to the beloved, not the bride; she asks to be drawn. Jesus's word in John 12:32 — 'when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself' — directly answers this petition: the drawing is accomplished through the cross. The entry 'into his chambers' anticipates the heavenly dwelling Jesus prepares (John 14:2-3) and the eschatological feast (Rev 19:9).
1.5: A revelation of God: 'I am dark and lovely' — the bride's self-description holds together two truths that seem contradictory: she is darkened by sun and labor (v. 6) yet loved and found beautiful. The NT's application is the church: sinful in herself, yet declared beautiful by the one who loved her and gave himself for her (Eph 5:25-27). The juxtaposition of darkness and loveliness is the aesthetic form of justification: the church is simultaneously sinful and beloved.
1.6: A revelation of God: The bride was made keeper of other vineyards while her own went unattended — a picture of the soul distracted from its own spiritual care by external demands. The NT parallel is Mary and Martha (Luke 10:38-42): Martha busy with service, missing the one thing necessary. Christ's response — 'you are anxious about many things; one thing is needed' — is the answer to the bride's complaint. The Beloved's prior claim on the soul precedes and grounds all other service.
1.7: A shadow: The bride asks where the shepherd rests his flock at midday — she wants to be where he is, not wandering among the flocks of others. This anticipates the disciple's question to Jesus: 'Rabbi, where are you staying?' (John 1:38), and Jesus's answer: 'Come and you will see.' The desire to be where Christ is rather than merely receiving his benefits is the mark of genuine faith — Paul's 'my desire is to depart and be with Christ' (Phil 1:23).
1.8: A shadow: 'Follow the tracks of the flock and pasture your young goats near the shepherds' tents.' The bride is directed to find the shepherd by following the flock already gathered around him. This reflects the NT's ecclesiology: the individual believer finds Christ by joining the assembled community that follows him (Heb 10:24-25). The path to intimacy with Christ runs through the people of Christ.
1.9: A revelation of God: The beloved is compared to a mare harnessed to Pharaoh's chariots — not the dainty ornamental horse of peacetime but the powerful animal that disrupts battle lines. The comparison speaks to the effect the beloved has on the lover: overwhelming, disruptive, captivating. God's delight in his people is depicted throughout Scripture as a love that is not cool or distant but deeply moved (Zeph 3:17: he will rejoice over you with gladness; he will quiet you by his love; he will exult over you with loud singing).
1.10: A revelation of God: The beloved's beauty is adorned with jewelry — cheeks beautiful with ornaments, neck with strings of beads. The theme of God adorning his people is developed in Ezekiel 16:11-14 (I adorned you with ornaments, put bracelets on your wrists, a chain on your neck) and finds its eschatological expression in the bride of the Lamb 'clothed with fine linen, bright and pure' (Rev 19:8). The adornment is not the bride's own achievement but the gift of the lover.
1.11: A revelation of God: 'We will make for you gold ornaments studded with silver' — the lover promises future adornment even more glorious than present gifts. The eschatological note: what Christ promises to the church is still ahead. The present partial beauty (1 Cor 13:12: now we see in a mirror dimly) will be replaced by the full adornment of the new creation. 'It has not yet appeared what we will be, but we know that when he appears we will be like him' (1 John 3:2).
1.12: A shadow: 'While the king reclined at his table, my perfume spread its fragrance.' The king at table — the meal scene — anticipates the eschatological banquet (Isa 25:6; Luke 14:15-24; Rev 19:9). The bride's fragrance filling the room while the king reclines suggests the mutual delight of the covenant relationship at its deepest: Christ and the church at table together. 'Behold, I stand at the door and knock. If anyone opens it, I will come in and eat with him' (Rev 3:20).
1.13: A shadow: The sachet of myrrh resting between the bride's breasts is a constant fragrance she carries with her. Myrrh in the OT is the burial spice (Gen 37:25; Exod 30:23) as well as the coronation anointing. In the NT, myrrh marks both the birth of Jesus (Matt 2:11: the magi's gift) and his burial (John 19:39: Nicodemus brought myrrh and aloes). The bride's carrying of myrrh against her heart foreshadows the church's carrying the cross of Christ — the atonement — as her central, constant treasure (1 Cor 2:2: I decided to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and him crucified).
1.14: A shadow: The cluster of henna blossoms from the vineyards of En-gedi — En-gedi was an oasis in the Judean wilderness, a place of lush growth in an arid landscape. The image of the beloved as an oasis in the desert is the OT's pervasive image for YHWH's provision in the wilderness (Exod 17:6; Isa 35:1-7; 43:19-20). Jesus applies the same image to himself: 'If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink' (John 7:37); 'I am the vine' (John 15:1). The cluster of blossoms is beauty and sweetness in a place of barrenness.
1.15: A revelation of God: 'How beautiful you are, my love! Your eyes are like doves.' The lover's repeated praise of the beloved's beauty — 'how beautiful' — is the divine delight in the people God has redeemed. Dove-eyes in the ancient world conveyed purity and singularity of focus. Jesus says 'the eye is the lamp of the body' (Matt 6:22) — a single eye focused on Christ is the covenant loyalty the Song celebrates. Christ's delight in the church's love for him (Rev 2:4: you have abandoned the love you had at first) is the reverse of this verse's joy.
1.16: A revelation of God: The bride praises the groom's beauty and declares their shared dwelling place — a bower of green. The mutuality: she finds him beautiful as he finds her. The church's growing capacity to see Christ as beautiful — not merely useful or powerful but personally lovely — is the mark of spiritual maturity (Phil 3:8: I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord).
1.17: A shadow: Cedar beams, cypress rafters — the finest timber of the ancient world (cf. the temple in 1 Kgs 6:15-18). The lovers' dwelling is built with the same materials as the house of God. The image points forward to the new creation where the dwelling of God is with his people (Rev 21:3): what the Song envisions as an intimate dwelling of lovers is the eschatological telos of redemption history — God and his people at home with one another.

3.1: A shadow: 'At night on my bed I searched for the one I love; I searched but did not find him.' The nocturnal seeking of the absent beloved is the church's experience of divine withdrawal — what the mystics called deus absconditus, the hidden God. The pattern is the structure of Holy Saturday between crucifixion and resurrection: the disciples' Beloved had gone and they did not know where. Mary Magdalene's night-seeking at the tomb (John 20:1: while it was still dark) directly parallels this opening. The seeking that does not yet find is not rejection but the discipline of desire.
3.2: A shadow: The bride rises and goes about the city — active, not passive, in her searching. The church does not wait for Christ to appear; she goes seeking. Jesus's parable of the woman seeking the lost coin (Luke 15:8-10) reverses the polarity: there Christ seeks the lost church. But here the church actively seeks Christ — the reciprocal dynamic of love. 'Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and it will be opened to you' (Matt 7:7). The seeking that persists will not ultimately fail.
3.3: A revelation of God: The watchmen who make their rounds through the city encounter the seeking bride. Watchmen in the OT are the prophets and spiritual leaders charged with guarding Israel (Ezek 3:17; 33:7). In the NT, the ministers of the Word are those who 'watch for your souls' (Heb 13:17). The bride's question — 'Have you seen the one I love?' — is the question the seeking soul asks of every resource available. The answer to her seeking comes not from the watchmen but from finding the Beloved himself (v. 4).
3.4: A type: 'I had barely passed them when I found the one I love. I held him and would not let him go until I brought him into my mother's house.' The finding and holding of the Beloved after the anguished search is the resurrection pattern. Mary Magdalene's encounter with the risen Christ — she turns, recognizes him, and grasps him (John 20:16-17) — is the NT's fulfillment of this scene. Jesus's word 'Do not cling to me' (John 20:17) addresses the very impulse the bride expresses: the love that cannot let go. The mother's house is the assembled church that receives the risen Christ.
3.5: A revelation of God: The adjuration not to awaken love prematurely (repeated from 2:7) marks a structural refrain in the Song — the divine sovereignty over the timing of the covenant relationship cannot be violated. In the NT, this timing is the Father's prerogative: 'concerning that day or that hour, no one knows ... but the Father only' (Matt 24:36). The repeated refrain teaches the church patience: the fullness of union comes in its proper time, not before.
3.6: A shadow: 'Who is this coming up from the desert like a column of smoke, fragrant with myrrh and frankincense?' The coming figure from the desert, wreathed in incense smoke, evokes Israel's wilderness journey (Exod 13:21-22: the pillar of cloud and fire) and the incense of the Tabernacle. In the NT, the one who comes from the wilderness is John the Baptist (Matt 3:1-3; Luke 1:80), the forerunner, and then Christ himself, who is led into the wilderness (Matt 4:1) and returns from it. The fragrant cloud is the presence of the Holy One.
3.7: A shadow: Sixty mighty warriors surrounding the royal litter — the finest men of Israel, swords at their sides, ready for the night. The image of the divine warrior surrounded by his guard is the OT's picture of the angelic host that accompanies YHWH (Ps 68:17: the chariots of God are ten thousand, two thousand repeated; Dan 7:10: a thousand thousands served him). The NT's parousia includes the angelic escort: 'the Son of Man comes in his glory and all the angels with him' (Matt 25:31).
3.8: A shadow: Skilled warriors, swords drawn against the dangers of the night — the protective force of the Beloved's company. The church is guarded not by her own strength but by the power that surrounds Christ. 'The angel of the LORD encamps around those who fear him' (Ps 34:7); 'he who is in you is greater than he who is in the world' (1 John 4:4). The night's dangers are real; the protection is absolute.
3.9: A shadow: King Solomon's royal litter (apiryon) from Lebanese cedar — the same timber as the Temple (1 Kgs 5:6-10). The litter built for the king's personal use with temple-quality materials signals that the King himself is a living sanctuary — his presence is the dwelling of God. The NT's claim is that Christ is the true temple (John 2:19-21: 'he was speaking about the temple of his body'), and those who are in Christ are built together as a dwelling place for God (Eph 2:21-22).
3.10: A type: Silver posts, gold back, purple seat — the Tabernacle material vocabulary: silver (the ransom silver of Exod 30:12-16), gold (the ark, the lampstand, the altar), purple (the priestly garments, Exod 26:1). The royal litter is constructed like a portable sanctuary. The correspondence is deliberate: the place where the king sits is a microcosm of the holy place. Christ is the one in whom all the fullness of the Deity dwells (Col 2:9); he is the new Tabernacle and the new temple simultaneously.
3.11: A shadow: 'Come out, daughters of Zion, and gaze on King Solomon wearing the crown with which his mother crowned him — on his wedding day, the day of the joy of his heart.' The wedding day of the king — crowned, joyful — is the eschatological day toward which all of redemption history moves. 'The marriage of the Lamb has come, and his Bride has made herself ready' (Rev 19:7). The daughters of Zion called to witness the king's joy are the church assembled to witness and participate in the final consummation. The joy is mutual: 'the day of the joy of his heart.'

4.1: A revelation of God: The groom's elaborate praise of the bride's beauty — 'how beautiful you are, my love! How beautiful!' — is the divine delight in the redeemed people. The repetition ('how beautiful ... how beautiful') is not poetic filler but intensity. God's delight in his people is not grudging or conditional: Zephaniah 3:17 describes YHWH exulting over his people with loud singing. The wasf form (descriptive poem from head to foot) communicates that the beloved is beautiful in every detail, not merely in general impression.
4.2: A revelation of God: Teeth like freshly washed sheep, each with its twin, none missing — the imagery of completeness and purity. The flock with 'not one missing' anticipates Christ's declaration: 'I have not lost one of those you gave me' (John 17:12; 18:9). The Good Shepherd does not lose a single sheep; the Bride presented to Christ has 'not one missing.' The eschatological completeness is already real in the divine intention.
4.3: A shadow: Lips like a scarlet thread — the scarlet of covenant and redemption. Rahab's scarlet cord in the window (Josh 2:18-21) is the OT's most explicit scarlet-as-salvation image; the Passover blood on the doorposts is its prototype. The beloved's lips are the color of covenant rescue. Her words — the 'lovely' mouth — are the praises of the redeemed, the confession of the lips that saves (Rom 10:9-10: with the mouth one confesses and is saved).
4.4: A shadow: 'Your neck is like the tower of David, built in courses of stone; a thousand shields hang on it, all of them warriors' shields.' The tower of David as armory — shields displayed as trophies of completed victories. The beloved's neck carries the history of war and triumph. The church's identity is constituted by what Christ has accomplished: the trophies of his cross (Col 2:14-15: disarming the rulers and authorities, he put them to open shame). The strength of the church is not her own but the victories already won and displayed.
4.5: A revelation of God: 'Your two breasts are like two young fawns, twins of a gazelle, feeding among the lilies.' The imagery of the twin fawns feeding peacefully evokes abundance, gentleness, and contentment. The pastoral scene — feeding in a place of beauty — is the Psalm 23 image: 'he makes me lie down in green pastures' (v. 2). God's delight in his people is marked by this same combination of protective strength and tender care. The fawns need no armor; they are in safe pasture.
4.6: A shadow: 'Until the day breaks and the shadows disappear, I will go to the mountain of myrrh and the hill of frankincense.' The mountain of myrrh is the mountain of Christ's sacrifice — Golgotha, where the King gives himself. Myrrh and frankincense are both burial spice (John 19:39) and incense of the holy place (Exod 30:23, 34). The groom's willingness to go there — before the day fully breaks — is the self-giving love of Christ who 'went to the mountain' of Calvary while it was still the dark hour of the world.
4.7: A direct citation of this theme: 'You are altogether beautiful, my love; there is no defect in you.' Paul quotes the structural equivalent in Ephesians 5:27: 'that he might present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle or any such thing, that she might be holy and without blemish.' The 'no defect' (mum in Hebrew — the same word used for Levitical sacrificial purity, Lev 22:21) is the standard Christ applies to the church in his sight. This is the most direct application of Song language to the Christ-church relationship in the NT.
4.8: A shadow: 'Come with me from Lebanon, my bride; come from Lebanon. Come down from the peak of Amana, from the summit of Senir and Hermon, from the dens of lions, from the mountains of leopards.' The call to leave the dangerous high places and come with the beloved is the gospel's call to leave the world's kingdoms for the kingdom of Christ. 'Come out of her, my people' (Rev 18:4) uses the same structure. Lebanon with its wild, dangerous heights is the world outside the protective love of the Beloved.
4.9: A revelation of God: 'You have stolen my heart, my sister, my bride; you have stolen my heart with a single glance.' The double address — 'sister, my bride' — combines kinship (covenant solidarity) and marriage (intimate love). The church is both family and bride to Christ: 'whoever does the will of my Father in heaven is my brother and sister and mother' (Matt 12:50) and 'the Bride, the wife of the Lamb' (Rev 21:9). A single glance of faith captivates the heart of God — this is the theology of grace: the smallest turning toward Christ draws the full response of divine love.
4.10: A revelation of God: 'How beautiful your love is, my sister, my bride! How much better is your love than wine.' The reversal: in 1:2 the bride said 'your love is better than wine'; now the groom says 'your love is better than wine.' The divine delight in the love of his people is genuine: God values the love of his people. John's vision of the lampstands (Rev 2-3) with the threat of removing the lampstand from the church that has 'abandoned the love you had at first' (Rev 2:4) shows this is not a poetic compliment but a theological truth: God treasures the love of his people.
4.11: A revelation of God: 'Honey and milk are under your tongue; the fragrance of your garments is like the fragrance of Lebanon.' The honey and milk under the tongue — the language of the promised land (Exod 3:8; 13:5) — applied to the bride herself. The church is the place where the promised land's abundance now resides. The NT equivalent: the community of the Spirit where the fruits of the Spirit (Gal 5:22-23: love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control) are present. The fragrance of Lebanon is the fresh purity of a life lived before God.
4.12: A shadow: 'My sister, my bride, is a garden locked, a spring sealed, a fountain closed.' The locked garden and sealed spring — the church as a place that is exclusive to Christ and not open to all. Paul expresses the same exclusivity in 2 Corinthians 11:2: 'I betrothed you to one husband, to present you as a pure virgin to Christ.' The church's distinctiveness in the world — her 'sealedness' — is not arrogance but covenant loyalty: she belongs to one Lord alone. The sealed spring will open when the Beloved enters (v. 16).
4.13: A shadow: The spice garden — pomegranates, henna, nard. The incense spice catalogue (v. 13-14) parallels the Tabernacle incense of Exodus 30:23-25: myrrh, cinnamon, calamus, cassia. The bride as a garden of holy incense spices evokes the holy place where God dwells. The NT applies this to the church: 'we are the aroma of Christ to God' (2 Cor 2:15); the prayers of the saints are incense before the throne (Rev 5:8). The garden-bride is the dwelling-place of the divine fragrance.
4.14: A shadow: Nard, saffron, calamus, cinnamon, frankincense, myrrh, aloes — the full incense catalogue of Exodus 30. The deliberate parallelism with the Tabernacle incense identifies the bride as a walking sanctuary, a living holy-of-holies. The NT's fulfillment: 'do you not know that you are God's temple and that God's Spirit dwells in you?' (1 Cor 3:16); 'you yourselves like living stones are being built up as a spiritual house, to offer spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ' (1 Pet 2:5).
4.15: A shadow: 'A garden fountain, a well of fresh water, streams flowing from Lebanon.' The enclosed garden of the bride has an inexhaustible water source. Jesus applied this imagery to himself: 'If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink ... as the Scripture has said, "Out of his heart will flow rivers of living water"' (John 7:37-38). The bride as a garden-fountain is, in the NT, the community from which living water flows to others — the church as the place where Christ's life is distributed to the world.
4.16: A shadow: 'Awake, O north wind, and come, O south wind! Blow through my garden and carry its fragrance abroad.' The bride's prayer for the winds to carry her fragrance abroad — the petition for the Spirit to disperse what is gathered in the closed garden into the wider world. This is the structure of Pentecost (Acts 2:2: a sound like a mighty rushing wind) and of the church's mission: 'you will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you, and you will be my witnesses ... to the end of the earth' (Acts 1:8). The garden opens to the world when the Spirit blows.

5.1: A shadow: 'I have come to my garden, my sister, my bride; I gathered my myrrh with my spice.' The groom enters the garden — the response to the bride's invitation in 4:16. The incarnation is this entering: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us (John 1:14). Christ comes into the human garden not as a visitor but as one who gathers and eats and drinks — the full participation in creaturely life. 'Eat, O friends, and drink: drink deeply, O beloved ones!' is the invitation to share in the abundance of the divine-human communion.
5.2: A shadow: 'I slept, but my heart was awake. A voice — my beloved knocking: Open to me.' The parable of the ten virgins (Matt 25:1-13) uses the same structure: some are ready when the bridegroom comes, some are not. Here the bride is half-asleep when the knock comes — not the failure of the foolish virgins but the spiritual drowsiness that Christ warns against (Mark 13:35-37: stay awake; you do not know when the master of the house will come). Christ's knock and voice at the door (Rev 3:20) is the same appeal as this midnight knocking: 'Open to me.'
5.3: A revelation of God: 'I have taken off my robe — how can I put it back on? I have washed my feet — how can I dirty them again?' The bride's hesitation — the cost of responding. She is comfortable; the Beloved's call requires getting up, getting dressed, dirtying the feet. Jesus's teaching on the cost of discipleship (Luke 9:23: 'take up your cross daily and follow me'; Luke 14:33: 'whoever of you does not renounce all that he has cannot be my disciple') is the same dynamic: the call of Christ requires inconvenience, disruption, the willingness to dirty the feet again for his sake.
5.4: A revelation of God: 'My beloved thrust his hand through the latch opening, and I felt a deep longing for him.' The persistence of Christ who comes even when the door is not immediately opened — 'I stand at the door and knock; if anyone hears my voice and opens the door, I will come in' (Rev 3:20). The bride's 'deep longing' is stirred by the very hesitation and delay: sometimes what awakens desire most forcefully is the moment when it was almost refused. Grace can work through the experience of near-loss.
5.5: A shadow: 'My hands dripped with myrrh — my fingers with flowing myrrh — on the handles of the bolt.' The myrrh on the handles is the fragrance of the Beloved's own hand — he has left his presence on the very door she finally opens. The myrrh of Christ's passion (the burial spice, John 19:39) marks the point of entry: the way into the Beloved's presence runs through the cross. The door of grace is the door marked with the sacrifice of Christ, just as the Passover doorposts were marked with blood (Exod 12:7).
5.6: A shadow: 'I opened to my beloved, but he had gone. I was beside myself when he left.' The opened door and the absent Beloved — the experience of seeking the risen Christ and finding the empty tomb. Mary Magdalene's grief at the empty tomb (John 20:11-13: they have taken away my Lord, and I do not know where they have laid him) is the direct NT parallel. The Beloved's withdrawal is not rejection but is a pattern of the divine-human relationship: moments of absence drive the seeking heart to find Christ in a new way.
5.7: A revelation of God: The watchmen who beat and wounded the seeking bride — the suffering of the church as she seeks Christ in a hostile world. The NT pattern is the Passion and Acts: the disciples scattered when the Shepherd was struck (Zech 13:7; Matt 26:31); Stephen, Paul, and the apostles bear wounds from those who guard the city's order while the church seeks the risen Lord. The stripping of the mantle (veil, honor) parallels the shaming of Christ's followers for their loyalty to him (Heb 13:13: let us go to him outside the camp, bearing his reproach).
5.8: A revelation of God: 'If you find my beloved, tell him I am faint with love.' The second use of this phrase (2:5 was 'I am faint with love' from the excess of his presence; 5:8 is the same faintness from his absence) — the same overwhelming love in both conditions. Paul's analogous statement: 'I am hard pressed between the two. My desire is to depart and be with Christ, for that is far better' (Phil 1:23). The faintness of love is not weakness but the sign that the relationship with Christ is the deepest center of the soul's life.
5.9: A revelation of God: The daughters of Jerusalem ask what makes the bride's beloved superior — 'what makes him so special that you beg this of us?' The question invites the wasf of 5:10-16 — the church's description of Christ to the world. This is the dynamic of evangelism: the world asks what makes Christ different from any other beloved, and the church answers with a description of his person and beauty. 'Always be prepared to give a reason for the hope that is in you' (1 Pet 3:15). The description begins with v. 10.
5.10: A revelation of God: 'My beloved is radiant and rugged, outstanding among ten thousand men.' The description of the Beloved begins at the top — radiant (dazzling white), rugged (ruddy, healthy strength). The NT's vision of the risen Christ (Rev 1:13-16: his face like the sun shining in full strength; his eyes like flames of fire) is the unveiled form of the beauty the bride describes. 'Outstanding among ten thousand' is the incomparability of Christ: there is no other mediator, no other Savior, no other name (Acts 4:12).
5.11: A revelation of God: 'His head is like purest gold; his hair is wavy and dark as a raven.' The gold head recalls the vision in Daniel 7:9 (the Ancient of Days whose hair was white as wool) and Revelation 1:14 (the Son of Man whose head and hair were white as white wool). The divine majesty expressed through the imagery of precious metal and perfect appearance: the one the bride describes is no ordinary beloved. The raven-dark hair in the earthly wasf becomes white as wool in the eschatological vision — the same person seen in two frames of time.
5.12: A revelation of God: 'His eyes are like doves beside streams of water — bathed in milk, perfectly set.' The dove-eyes of 1:15 (where the bride was praised with this image) are now attributed to the Beloved — the mutuality of the love is mirrored in the imagery. The eyes of Christ are his attention to his people: 'the eyes of the LORD are toward the righteous and his ears toward their cry' (Ps 34:15). The eyes 'bathed in milk' convey purity and clarity of vision — the all-seeing gaze that is not surveillance but pure love.
5.13: A revelation of God: 'His cheeks are like beds of spice, banks of sweet herbs. His lips are lilies dripping with flowing myrrh.' The beloved's lips are lilies dripping myrrh — beauty and sacrifice together. The words of Christ are 'grace and truth' (John 1:17) — the grace of the lily's beauty, the truth of the myrrh's honesty about suffering. 'No one ever spoke like this man' (John 7:46). The lips of the beloved produce words that are simultaneously fragrant (grace) and sacrificial (myrrh).
5.14: A shadow: Gold cylinders set with jewels, carved ivory overlaid with sapphires — the materials of the Tabernacle and Temple: gold, precious stones, ivory. The beloved's body described in Tabernacle terms: he is the dwelling-place of God in visible form. The NT's direct statement: 'in him all the fullness of the Deity dwells bodily' (Col 2:9). The beloved described as a walking sanctuary is the shadow the incarnation brings to its fulfillment.
5.15: A shadow: 'His legs are like columns of alabaster, set in sockets of gold. His form is like Lebanon — magnificent as the cedars.' The alabaster columns set in gold evoke the temple columns (1 Kgs 7:15-22: the two bronze pillars Jachin and Boaz set at the entrance to the temple). The Beloved's form is the form of the holy place. John 2:19-21 is the interpretive key: the temple is his body. What the Solomonic temple embodied architecturally, Christ embodied personally — the dwelling of God with humanity.
5.16: A revelation of God: 'His mouth is sweetness itself; he is altogether desirable. This is my beloved and this is my friend, O daughters of Jerusalem.' The climax of the wasf: mouth = sweetness, whole person = altogether desirable, and the double identification: my beloved AND my friend. The NT expresses the same double identity: 'I no longer call you servants ... I have called you friends' (John 15:15). Christ is not only the majestic Lord whose description fills vv. 10-16 but also the intimate Friend whose knowledge of us is complete and whose love is personal.

6.1: A revelation of God: The daughters of Jerusalem now ask where the beloved has gone — they want to help find him. The community that was curious (5:9) is now engaged. The church's testimony about Christ — her description of his beauty in 5:10-16 — has drawn others to seek him. This is the dynamic of witness: when the church speaks authentically about the person of Christ, others are drawn to seek him for themselves. The watching world joins the search.
6.2: A revelation of God: 'My beloved has gone to his garden, to the beds of spices, to graze in the gardens and gather lilies.' The bride knows where the Beloved is — in the garden, gathering lilies (his own beloved ones, described as lilies in 2:2). Even in his apparent absence he is at work gathering. Christ's absence from the earth between ascension and parousia is not inactivity: he is interceding (Heb 7:25), building (Matt 16:18), and gathering (John 11:52: to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad).
6.3: A revelation of God: 'I am my beloved's and he is mine; he grazes among the lilies.' The mutual possession formula of 2:16 is reversed: in 2:16, the bride says 'my beloved is mine and I am his' — her possession stated first. Here, 'I am my beloved's' comes first — his claim on her precedes her claim on him. The reversal marks spiritual maturation: the soul that grows in Christ-likeness moves from thinking primarily of Christ as 'mine' to understanding herself primarily as 'his.' The possessive order is the theology of sanctification.
6.4: A revelation of God: 'You are beautiful as Tirzah, my love, lovely as Jerusalem, awe-inspiring as an army under banners.' The city-comparisons: Tirzah (the northern capital, later), Jerusalem (the southern capital, city of David). The church is beautiful as the great cities — the centers of civilization and culture. But the final comparison is unexpected: awe-inspiring as an army with banners. The church's beauty is not merely decorative but military — she is a force that inspires awe. Ephesians 6:10-17 (the armor of God) is the NT's development: the church that is beautiful is also equipped for war.
6.5: A revelation of God: 'Look away — your eyes overwhelm me!' The beloved's eyes are so powerful that the groom asks her to look away — her focused love is overwhelming. The intensity of the mutual gaze: Christ is overwhelmed by the love of his people? This is the language of divine delight, the same astonishment that marks God's covenant love: 'Can a woman forget her nursing child ... Even these may forget, yet I will not forget you' (Isa 49:15). The divine-human love relationship is one in which the Creator is genuinely moved by the creature's love.
6.6: A revelation of God: The second wasf (6:5b-7) repeats elements from the first (4:1-3) — hair like a flock of goats, teeth like washed sheep with twins, none missing. The repetition is not artistic laziness but theological insistence: the church's completeness and purity before Christ does not change. The eschatological promise — 'not one missing' — is reaffirmed after the episode of seeking and striving (ch. 5). The bride is no less beautiful after the night of seeking than before.
6.7: A revelation of God: The cheeks like pomegranate halves behind the veil — the veil that conceals while suggesting. The church's beauty is partially veiled in this age: 'now we see in a mirror dimly, but then face to face' (1 Cor 13:12). What the veil now hides will be fully revealed in the eschaton: 'when Christ who is your life appears, you also will appear with him in glory' (Col 3:4). The pomegranate's rich red behind the veil anticipates the full color of the unveiled face.
6.8: A revelation of God: Sixty queens, eighty concubines, maidens beyond counting — the vast retinue of the ancient king. The contrast with the unique dove will follow in v. 9. The numbers anticipate the incomparability of the bride: not that others are without value, but that this one is singular. The NT applies this to the church: 'There is one body and one Spirit ... one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God and Father of all' (Eph 4:4-6). The uniqueness of the relationship between Christ and the church is not diminished by the existence of others.
6.9: A revelation of God: 'My dove, my flawless one, is unique — her mother's only daughter, treasured by the one who gave birth to her.' The word translated 'flawless' is tam — the same word used of Job (1:1: blameless and upright) and of the Passover lamb (Exod 12:5: without blemish). The church is unique and without blemish before Christ — not because of her inherent perfection but because of what Christ has done: 'he has now reconciled you in his body of flesh by his death, in order to present you holy and blameless and above reproach before him' (Col 1:22).
6.10: A shadow: 'Who is this who appears like the dawn, beautiful as the moon, bright as the sun, awe-inspiring as an army with banners?' The fourfold description climaxing in 'bright as the sun' — the eschatological glory of the church in her consummated state. Revelation 12:1 applies the same imagery to the woman clothed with the sun: the church in her heavenly glory. 'Then the righteous will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father' (Matt 13:43, drawing on Dan 12:3). What the Song describes in the language of natural light the NT promises as the final reality.
6.11: A revelation of God: 'I went down to the grove of walnut trees to look at the new growth in the valley, to see whether the vines had budded or the pomegranates were in bloom.' The beloved's inspection of the garden for signs of new life — the tender examination of fruitfulness. Christ is the one who comes looking for fruit: the parable of the fig tree in Luke 13:6-9 (the owner who comes looking and finds no fruit, but gives one more season). His attention to growth and fruitfulness in the church is watchful and patient.
6.12: A revelation of God: 'Before I realized it, my longing had placed me beside my noble kinsman's chariot.' The unexpected transport — longing that moves faster than thought. The spiritual experience of being caught up into nearness with Christ unexpectedly: Paul's 'caught up to the third heaven' (2 Cor 12:2: whether in the body or out of the body I do not know — God knows). The soul's longing for Christ sometimes outstrips its own comprehension and finds itself in his presence before it intended to seek him.
6.13: A shadow: 'Come back, come back, O Shulamite; come back, come back, so we can look at you!' The calling back of the Shulamite — the word Shulamit is the feminine form of Shalom, the woman of peace and wholeness. The church called back from the ends of the earth: 'Come, Lord Jesus' (Rev 22:20) is the church's cry; but 'Come back, come back, O Shulamite' is the divine call to the bride to return. The final exchange — 'why do you gaze on the Shulamite as at the dance of Mahanaim?' — evokes the two camps of angels at Mahanaim (Gen 32:1-2): the church is beautiful and wondrous to the heavenly hosts, who long to look into the things of salvation (1 Pet 1:12).
